1: Psalm 135 is one of the most anthological psalms in the Psalter — it is woven together from phrases found throughout the Torah, Former Prophets, and other psalms (especially Ps 115 and 136). This intertextuality was probably intentional liturgical composition: by weaving together earlier sacred texts, the psalm creates a concentrated summary of covenant theology suitable for temple worship. The opening call "Praise the LORD; praise the name of the LORD" (hallĕlû šēm-YHWH) introduces the "name" (šēm) as the psalm's theological center — YHWH's self-disclosure and character, which the psalm will celebrate through his works in creation and history.
2: "You who stand in the house of the LORD, in the courts of the house of our God" — the address specifies the audience: the temple personnel serving in the courts. The "house of the LORD" and "courts of the house of our God" reflect the temple's spatial structure — outer courts accessible to all Israel, inner courts restricted to priests and Levites. The congregation standing in the outer courts and the Levites in the inner courts are all addressed. This liturgical specificity anchors the universal theology of the psalm (YHWH sovereign over all nations) in the concrete worship assembly of YHWH's particular people.
3: "Praise the LORD, for the LORD is good; sing praise to his name, for that is pleasant (nāʿîm)" — the double motivation for praise: YHWH's goodness (ṭôb) and the pleasantness (nāʿîm) of praising his name. Nāʿîm (pleasant, lovely, delightful) applies both to the experience of worship itself and to YHWH's character. The word also appears in Ps 133:1 ("How good and pleasant it is when God's people live together in unity") — the same root that describes covenantal community harmony now applied to covenantal praise. Worship is not merely obligatory but genuinely pleasant — an experience worth having for its own sake.
4: "For the LORD has chosen Jacob to be his own, Israel to be his treasured possession (sĕgullāh)" — Deut 7:6 almost verbatim: "the LORD your God has chosen you out of all the peoples on the face of the earth to be his people, his treasured possession." Sĕgullāh (treasured possession, special property) was an ANE term for the personal property of a king held separate from state treasury — a private treasure. Exod 19:5 uses it in YHWH's covenant address: "you will be my treasured possession (sĕgullāh) out of all nations." Israel is YHWH's personally held treasure — the basis of election, not a reward for merit.
5: "I know that the LORD is great, that our Lord is greater than all gods" — the henotheistic confession that moves toward monotheism. The formula "greater than all gods" acknowledges the existence of rival deities in the worldview of surrounding cultures while asserting YHWH's superiority over all of them. This formula appears also in Exod 18:11 (Jethro's confession), Ps 96:4-5, and Ps 97:9. The progression in the Psalter is from this henotheistic formula toward explicit monotheism (Isa 44:6-8; 45:5-6) — one God, not merely the greatest among many.
6: "The LORD does whatever pleases him (kōl-ʾăšer-ḥāpēṣ ʿāśāh), in the heavens and on the earth, in the seas and all their depths" — the formula of unrestricted divine sovereignty. No domain limits YHWH's action: heavens (the realm of the divine council and cosmic powers), earth (the historical realm of nations), seas (the chaos waters in ANE cosmology), and their depths (kol-tĕhômôt — the primordial abyss). This verse is almost exactly repeated in Ps 115:3 and echoed in Jonah 1:14 ("You, LORD, have done as it pleased you"). The freedom of divine action — doing "whatever pleases him" — is the foundation of the psalm's celebration of YHWH's historical acts.
7: "He makes clouds rise from the ends of the earth; he sends lightning with the rain and brings out the wind from his storehouses" — YHWH as controller of the weather cycle, a polemic against Baal (the storm-god) and Hadad (the rain-deity) of Canaanite religion. Jer 10:13 and 51:16 use nearly identical language ("He makes clouds rise from the ends of the earth"). The "storehouses (ʾôṣĕrôt)" of wind and rain are the divine treasury from which weather is dispensed (Job 38:22-23 — YHWH's storehouses of snow, hail, wind). By controlling the weather cycle, YHWH controls the agricultural calendar — the power Canaanite farmers attributed to Baal.
8: "He struck down the firstborn of Egypt, the firstborn of people and animals" — the tenth plague (Exod 12:29) cited as the premier example of YHWH's historical action. The psalm moves from creation sovereignty (vv. 6-7) to historical sovereignty (vv. 8-12), following the same pattern as Ps 136. The inclusion of "animals" alongside people specifies the totality of the plague (Exod 12:29 includes "firstborn of livestock"). In ANE theology, the firstborn of animals were also sacred — the Passover sacrifice of a firstborn lamb (Exod 12:3-5) was specifically the substitution of Israel's firstborn through the blood of a firstborn animal.
9: "He sent signs and wonders into your midst, Egypt, against Pharaoh and all his servants" — "signs and wonders (ʾôtôt ûmôpĕtîm)" is the technical formula for the Exodus miracles (Deut 4:34; 6:22; 7:19; 26:8). "Against Pharaoh and all his servants" — the wonders targeted the royal house specifically (as in Exod 11:5 — all the firstborn "from the firstborn of Pharaoh, who sits on the throne, to the firstborn of the female slave") because Pharaoh was the divine representative of Egyptian sovereignty, and YHWH's contest was with the Egyptian cosmic order, not merely its military power.
10: "He struck down many nations and killed mighty kings" — the transition from the Exodus plagues to the Transjordan and Canaanite battles. "Many nations" and "mighty kings" are summarizing language for the conquest narratives (Num 21; Josh 12 lists 31 defeated Canaanite kings). The movement in vv. 8-12 recapitulates the Pentateuch and Joshua: Egypt (v. 8-9) → Wilderness/Transjordan (vv. 10-11) → Canaan/inheritance (v. 12). The same sequence as the Exodus-to-Settlement narrative arc confirms that the land promise drove all of YHWH's historical acts.
11: "Sihon king of the Amorites and Og king of Bashan, and all the kingdoms of Canaan" — Sihon and Og became paradigmatic examples of divine victory (Num 21:21-35; Deut 2:26-3:11). They are cited together in at least twelve passages (Num 21; Deut 1, 2, 3, 4, 29, 31; Josh 2, 9, 12; Ps 136:19-20; Neh 9:22). Sihon ruled the fertile Transjordan plateau east of the Jordan; Og ruled Bashan (the modern Golan Heights), famous for its fortified cities and its king's enormous iron bed (Deut 3:11). Their defeat opened the land of Canaan for settlement. The pairing "Amorites and Bashan" would have been immediately recognizable to any Israelite worshiper.
12: "And gave their land as an inheritance (naḥălāh), an inheritance to his people Israel" — the land-as-inheritance theology (see Ps 105:11; Deut 12:9; Josh 11:23). Naḥălāh (inheritance, patrimony) emphasizes that the land is not simply conquered territory but a family estate given by divine gift through the covenant. The doubling of naḥălāh in this verse (inherited land given as inheritance) is emphatic: the whole point of the historical acts (vv. 8-11) was this — the gift of a place to belong, a homeland for the covenant people. The land as covenant gift underlies the NT theology of a new inheritance (1 Pet 1:4; Heb 9:15).
13: "Your name, O LORD, endures forever; your renown, O LORD, endures through all generations" — a reflection on YHWH's permanent reputation in history. The "name (šēm)" is YHWH's covenantal self-disclosure, his character as revealed in act; the "renown (zēker)" is the memorial by which he is known — the remembrance of his deeds. Exod 3:15 establishes the connection: the divine name "YHWH" is "my memorial (zikrî) for all generations." The permanence of the divine name means the permanent accessibility of covenant relationship across time — each generation encounters the same God who acted in Egypt and Canaan.
14: "For the LORD will vindicate (yāḏîn) his people and have compassion (yitnĕḥam) on his servants" — a covenant-legal promise. "Vindicate (dîn)" — YHWH will judge on behalf of his people, provide legal advocacy. This recalls Deut 32:36 (nearly verbatim), which is also quoted in Heb 10:30 in the context of the divine judgment that falls on those who spurn the covenant. "Have compassion" — nāḥam (comfort, relent, have compassion) is the same word as the divine "relenting" in Exod 32:14. The combination — legal vindication and compassionate relenting — describes the full character of the covenant Judge: rigorously just and deeply compassionate.
15: "The idols of the nations are silver and gold, made by human hands" — this verse begins an idol polemic (vv. 15-18) that is nearly verbatim from Ps 115:4-8. The reuse may be intentional: by repeating the well-known Ps 115 polemic, Ps 135 creates a liturgical echo within the temple worship context. The "silver and gold" of the idols (precious materials) contrasts with YHWH who "made heaven and earth" (v. 6) and "made clouds rise" (v. 7). The idols' value is in their material; YHWH's value is in his acts. Made by human hands (maʿăśēh yĕdê ʾādām) — the critique: any god that can be manufactured is not ultimate.
16: "They have mouths, but cannot speak; eyes, but cannot see" — the idol polemic continues (Ps 115:5 verbatim). The six sense faculties listed (mouth, eyes, ears, nose in v. 17, hands, feet implied) demonstrate that the idols have the form of persons without the function of persons. In ANE belief, the ritual of "opening the mouth" (pīt pî) was performed on cult statues to animate them and make them capable of eating, speaking, and receiving worship. The psalm denies that any such animation is possible — the forms without function are permanently inert. Isa 44:9-20 develops this polemic most extensively.
17: "They have ears, but cannot hear; nor is there any breath (rûaḥ) in their mouths" — the breath-of-life test. Rûaḥ (breath, spirit, wind) is what distinguishes the living from the dead — Gen 2:7 (YHWH breathed the breath of life into Adam) and Ezek 37:1-14 (the valley of dry bones animated by the rûaḥ). The idols have no rûaḥ — they are as lifeless as the materials from which they were made. This is the decisive test in the contrast between YHWH and idols: YHWH is the source and giver of breath/spirit; idols can neither receive nor give it. The life-test reduces all idolatry to absurdity.
18: "Those who make them will be like them, and so will all who trust in them" — the climax of the idol polemic (identical to Ps 115:8). The makers and worshipers of idols become like what they worship — gradually losing the capacity for true perception, speech, and breath that the idols themselves lack. This is a principle of conformity in worship: we become what we worship. Paul develops this in Rom 1:21-25 (the idolater's "thinking became futile and their foolish hearts were darkened") and 2 Cor 3:18 (those who contemplate the glory of God "are being transformed into his image"). The inverse of idol-conformity is the transformation into divine likeness through worship of the living God.
19: "Praise the LORD, O house of Israel; praise the LORD, O house of Aaron" — the call-and-response liturgy that closes the psalm (vv. 19-20) mirrors Ps 115:9-11 and 118:2-4 almost verbatim. The three groups — house of Israel (all the people), house of Aaron (the high priests), house of Levi (the broader Levitical order in v. 20) — represent concentric circles of covenant obligation. The house of Israel constitutes the whole covenant assembly; the house of Aaron represents the priestly mediators; the house of Levi the larger serving class. The three-part structure mirrors the spatial organization of the temple (outer court, inner court, Levitical gatekeepers).
20: "Praise the LORD, O house of Levi; you who fear the LORD, praise the LORD" — the fourth group, "those who fear the LORD (yirĕʾê YHWH)," may refer to proselytes, God-fearers among the Gentiles, or simply all pious Israelites who do not fall into the priestly categories. The term appears in Ps 115:11 and 118:4 in the same liturgical position. In Second Temple Judaism, "those who fear the LORD" was applied to Gentile sympathizers who attached themselves to synagogue worship without full conversion (Acts 10:2 — Cornelius is described as "a devout man who feared God"). The psalm's inclusive ending hints at a universalizing direction for covenant worship.
21: "Blessed be the LORD from Zion, he who dwells in Jerusalem. Praise the LORD (hallĕlû-yāh)!" — the closing doxology matches the form of Ps 134:3: YHWH blessed "from Zion." The geographic specificity — "from Zion," "in Jerusalem" — grounds the psalm's cosmic theology (YHWH sovereign over heaven, earth, and seas) in the particular sanctuary where YHWH "dwells (šōkēn)." The šōkēn vocabulary (dwelling, tabernacling) is related to the Deuteronomic theology of YHWH "causing his name to dwell (šākan)" at the chosen place (Deut 12:11). The Hallĕlû-yāh closes the psalm as an echo of its opening.
